nativeRange.habitat: Shallow reef slopes, lagoons, and back-reef zones; commonly found encrusting on rocky substrate and dead coral skeletons in moderate-light, moderate-flow environments

decorPreferences: spacing from neighboring corals (5–8 cm minimum)

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via symbiotic zooxanthellae. Has been known to benefit from occasional supplemental feeding of small zooplankton (rotifers ~0.5 mm, copepods, cyclops 1–2 mm) or amino acid solutions a few times per week. Target feeding via pipette during low-flow periods or after lights out tends to produce better uptake results.

goodWith: non-aggressive LPS corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: corals placed within sweeper tentacle reach (less than 5–8 cm)
aggressive LPS corals with long sweepers (frogspawn, torch, hammer)

summary: Cyphastrea is an encrusting LPS/SPS-borderline coral prized for its vivid color morphs and beginner-friendly hardiness. Generally considered one of the easier stony corals, tolerating moderate light and stable reef parameters.
careNotes:
Note: Cyphastrea is a genus of roughly 12 recognized species (family Merulinidae, formerly Faviidae). The aquarium trade typically sells them at the genus level as 'Cyphastrea sp.' because husbandry requirements are near-identical across species. Common trade varieties include the 'Meteor Shower' morph (blue corallites with orange-red centers) and various solid-colored encrusting forms.

Classification notes: Cyphastrea sits on the LPS/SPS borderline. Individual polyps are small (SPS-like), but the coral is typically categorized and sold as LPS due to its fleshy tissue coverage and comparatively forgiving water-parameter tolerances. Care requirements are closer to those of intermediate LPS corals than true SPS.

Lighting: Sources broadly agree on a starting PAR of 100–150, with tolerance up to ~250 PAR once acclimated. New frags should be placed in lower, shadier positions and moved up very slowly — Cyphastrea is prone to bleaching under sudden high-light exposure. Recommended acclimation: increase PAR by no more than 25–50 units per week.

Flow: Moderate, indirect flow is consistently recommended across all sources. Direct high-flow currents can prevent polyp extension and cause tissue damage; flow should circulate around the colony rather than blasting it directly. This is especially important for smaller frags.

Aggression: Cyphastrea extends sweeper tentacles at night. Most sources describe the reach as relatively short compared to aggressive LPS (acan lords, frogspawn), but 5–8 cm clearance from neighbors is generally advised to avoid chemical or physical allelopathy as the colony encrusts outward.

Water quality: Calcium, alkalinity, and magnesium supplementation (via calcium reactor, kalkwasser, or two-part dosing) is required for sustained skeletal growth. Stable alkalinity is particularly important; large swings can stress the colony even if mean values are within range. Moderate nutrient levels (nitrate 5–10 ppm, phosphate 0.01–0.05 ppm) have been reported to support healthy coloration; ultra-low-nutrient systems may cause browning.

Temperature disagreement: LiveAquaria lists 72–78°F (22–26°C) while ReefChasers lists 77–84°F (25–29°C). The averaged range of 23–27°C is used here; the upper bound from one source (29°C) is considered an outlier for standard reef husbandry.

Growth: Cyphastrea is generally regarded as a fast-growing encrusting coral. Reports indicate 2–4 inches (5–10 cm) of lateral expansion per year under good conditions. Colonies can cover significant rock or glass surface area over time. Fragging is straightforward with a bandsaw.

Breeding: Reproduces sexually on the reef via broadcast spawning timed to tidal and lunar cycles. In captivity, propagation is almost exclusively via fragging.
breedingNotes: Fragging is the standard captive propagation method. Colonies can be cut with a tile saw or bandsaw; frags readily encrust and grow on frag plugs or rubble substrate. Sexual reproduction occurs via broadcast spawning on natural reefs, synchronized to lunar and tidal cycles, but deliberate captive sexual breeding is not practiced in the hobby.

primary.name: LiveAquaria — CCGC Aquacultured Red and Orange Cyphastrea Coral

additional.name: Tidal Gardens — Cyphastrea Coral Care
additional.notes: Cross-checked calcium (~425 ppm), alkalinity (8–9 dKH), magnesium (~1350 ppm), lighting (under 100 PAR start, up to 150 medium), flow (moderate indirect), native range (Australia, Indonesia). Care level described as beginner-friendly / one of the easiest stony corals.

additional.name: Reef Chasers — Cyphastrea Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (77–84°F; outlier high end not adopted), pH (8.0–8.4), alkalinity (8–9 dKH), calcium (400–450 ppm), PAR (100–200). Care level: beginner. Confirmed semi-aggressive sweeper tentacle behavior.

additional.name: Top Shelf Aquatics — Cyphastrea Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (75–82°F), salinity (1.023–1.025), calcium (420–450 ppm), alkalinity (8–9 dKH), magnesium (1350–1450 ppm), PAR (50–200). Confirms moderate indirect flow and 2–3 inch sweeper-tentacle spacing requirement.

additional.name: Build Your Aquarium — Cyphastrea Coral
additional.notes: Cross-checked native range (Indo-Pacific, Red Sea, East Africa, Australia, Philippines), temperature (72–78°F), salinity (1.023–1.025), alkalinity (9–12 dKH), calcium (350–450 ppm), magnesium (1200–1350 ppm). Notes sweeper tentacles as 'very short reach' compared to acan lords and frogspawn.
